cataloging_subject.label: The Four Sons of Aymon
cataloging_subject.scope_note: William Caxton's Middle English prose translation of the anonymous fifteenth-century French prose Les quatre fils Aymon, made and first printed at Westminster about 1490. The cataloged work comprises Caxton's prologue, the table of twenty-eight chapters, and the twenty-eight-chapter narrative. It excludes Octavia Richardson's introduction and plot sketch, her French collation apparatus, her supplied readings from William Copland's 1554 print, the EETS glossary and name index, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

notes: This concise established title distinguishes Caxton's English work from its French prose source while avoiding promotion of a title-page wording that survives only in a later print.

titles.value: The Right Plesaunt and Goodly Historie of the Foure Sonnes of Aymon
notes: The long wording is established by Copland's 1554 title and Richardson's edition; the surviving Caxton material lacks its opening title matter and colophon.
A current catalog uniform title for the English prose version; it does not imply that Caxton translated the verse chanson directly.

agents.name: Anonymous author of the French prose Les quatre fils Aymon
notes: The prose adaptation belongs to the fifteenth-century French Renaut de Montauban tradition; no individual author is established.

agents.name: William Caxton
notes: c. 1422-1491/1492; translator and first printer of the cataloged English work.

agents.name: John, Earl of Oxford
notes: Named in Caxton's prologue and current incunable catalogs as the person at whose desire the translation was undertaken; this record does not normalize the title to a more specific personal identity without authority evidence.

agents.name: Octavia Richardson
notes: Editor of EETS Extra Series 44-45, issued in 1884-1885 with title-page dates 1885-1886.

scope.description: Authorship or adaptation of the anonymous French prose source work translated by Caxton

notes: This is source-work responsibility, not authorship of Caxton's Middle English version.
Richardson characterizes the translation as notably literal; no separate adapter role is asserted here.

scope.description: Requester or commissioner of Caxton's English translation

notes: The controlled role list has no requester or commissioner value, so the exact function is retained in the scope.

scope.description: Composite transcription of the surviving Caxton copy, with explicitly marked passages and headings supplied from British Library C.12.i.7, Copland's 1554 edition

notes: The exact source switches are recorded as separately scoped relations in the lineage manifest; this responsibility must not be read as a homogeneous transcription from one copy.

scope.description: French comparison apparatus throughout the edited English text, using the British Museum copy now British Library IB.42244

notes: The comparison copy is identifiable from Richardson's repository, date, format, extent, and text description. Its use for collation does not prove that it was Caxton's physical exemplar.
The prologue joins translation and printing in one project, but gives no exact completion date. Richardson and the XML use about 1489; current ISTC and Cambridge cataloging use about 1490.
The print lacks printer, place, and date statements; the modern catalog date is approximate.
The printing place is not promoted to a separately proven place of translation.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML header's generic eng code is not sufficiently specific. French in Richardson's footnotes belongs to editorial apparatus and the source tradition, not to the intrinsic language of Caxton's English work.

form_statements.description: Caxton's prologue, table, and twenty-eight narrative chapters are prose. All fifteen L elements in the repository XML occur in Richardson's introduction, outside the cataloged work.

scope.description: The concluding movement of the narrative, especially Chapters XXIII-XXVIII

subject_statements.label: Magic, enchantment, Maugis, and the horse Bayard

summaries.value: After Duke Aymon's sons Reynawde, Alarde, Guycharde, and Rycharde become Charlemagne's knights, a chessboard killing and the Emperor's vengeance drive them into rebellion and exile. Aided by their cousin Maugis and the extraordinary horse Bayard, they build and defend fortresses, survive betrayals and sieges, and repeatedly resist Charlemagne before a negotiated reconciliation. Reynawde later makes pilgrimage to the Holy Land, relinquishes his possessions, works anonymously on the church of Saint Peter at Cologne, is murdered by jealous laborers, and is revealed through miracles as Saint Reynawde the martyr.

The work has a prose prologue, a table, and twenty-eight prose chapters. The repository XML represents these as thirty DIV2 containers: one prologue, one table, and twenty-eight chapter divisions. Richardson's source edition prints the cataloged work on pages 3-592; its introduction, plot sketch, glossarial index, and name index are editorial matter outside the work.

Richardson's EETS text is composite rather than a homogeneous transcription: the Manchester Caxton copy begins only at Richardson page 24, has three material internal losses, and has a small cut-out at page 591. Richardson supplied those passages, two missing chapter headings, and a final four-word repair from British Library C.12.i.7, William Copland's 1554 print. The exact switches belong to source lineage and do not make Copland a coauthor or make Richardson's additions intrinsic alternate forms of Caxton's work.

extent.description: The table and narrative enumerate twenty-eight chapters. The three top-level content parts are Caxton's prologue, the chapter table, and the narrative. The XML has thirty target-work DIV2 elements because the prologue and table precede the twenty-eight chapter divisions. All fifteen XML L elements occur in Richardson's introduction, outside the cataloged work.

content_parts.label: Caxton's prologue
content_parts.summary: Caxton commends knowledge of noble histories, recalls an earlier translation for the Earl of Oxford, states that the Earl sent him the French Four Sons of Aymon and requested an English version, and asks readers to correct faults.

locators.value: pp. 3-4; the opening precedes the first XML PB milestone, numbered 4

content_parts.label: Table of twenty-eight chapters
content_parts.summary: A prose table outlines the twenty-eight chapters from Aymon's sons at Charlemagne's court through Reynawde's penitential death and miracles.

content_parts.label: Twenty-eight-chapter romance narrative
content_parts.summary: Twenty-eight chapters narrate the sons' conflict with Charlemagne, exile, fortresses and sieges, adventures with Maugis and Bayard, reconciliation, and Reynawde's pilgrimage, penance, martyrdom, and miracles.

locators.value: pp. 321-592; continuation of Chapter XIV through Chapter XXVIII and AMEN
canvases 11-544 preserve the surviving Caxton text from Chapter I, Richardson p. 24 line 12, through AMEN, with documented internal losses

related_works.title: Les quatre fils Aymon (fifteenth-century French prose version)
notes: Caxton translated an unidentified physical copy of this prose version. The translation claim is evidenced for the twenty-eight-chapter romance narrative; Caxton's prologue is authorial, and the table's derivation from the exemplar is not independently established.
Hellinga identifies two Lyon printings as plausible alternatives but states that the exact edition cannot presently be decided; neither is identified as his exemplar here.

related_works.title: Renaut de Montauban (Old French chanson de geste)
notes: The chanson is the broader narrative ancestor mediated through the French prose adaptation, not Caxton's direct copytext.

evidence.citation: aha2639 XML source, FILEDESC and title page, in the pinned CME submodule.
evidence.summary: Identifies AHA2639, Richardson's two-volume EETS source, the catalog publication span 1884-85, the concatenated title-page dates MDCCCLXXXV and MDCCCLXXXVI, U-M electronic production in 2006, and the legacy availability statement.

evidence.citation: aha2639 XML source, Richardson introduction and Caxton prologue, table, and narrative.
evidence.summary: Supports the work boundary, Caxton's translation and requester statements, French prose ancestry, Middle English prose form, twenty-eight-chapter structure, plot, genres, subjects, source switches, and Richardson's French apparatus.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of the aha2639 repository XML.
evidence.locator: TEXT/BODY element counts and DIV1/DIV2 boundaries
evidence.summary: The XML has three DIV1 divisions: Richardson's introduction, her plot sketch, and the target work. The target DIV1 has thirty DIV2 children: prologue, table, and twenty-eight chapters. All fifteen L elements are in the introduction; the target has 439 P, 1,550 NOTE1, and no L elements.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, AHA2639 item information.
evidence.locator: title, creators, publication, rights, contents, and entire-text action
evidence.summary: The current item page identifies Caxton, Richardson, the 1884-85 Trübner edition, the encoded text, and Public Domain / No Copyright-United States rights presentation.

evidence.citation: Octavia Richardson, ed., The Right Plesaunt and Goodly Historie of the Foure Sonnes of Aymon, EETS Extra Series 44-45 (London: N. Trübner, issued 1884-1885).
evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. v-xvi; story sketch pp. xvii-xxix; target work pp. 3-592; glossarial index pp. 593-624; name index pp. 625-667
evidence.summary: The complete combined scan establishes Richardson's source discussion, work structure and pagination, French comparison practice, exact Caxton/Copland switches, title-page date 1885 for the preserved first part, and the distinction between work text and editorial back matter.

evidence.citation: Early English Text Society, List of Publications 2026, Extra Series 44-45.
evidence.summary: Lists The Foure Sons of Aymon Part 1, ES 44, as 1884 and Part 2, ES 45, as 1885, with separate current ISBNs and prices. These are issue years and are retained separately from the 1885 and 1886 title-page evidence.

evidence.citation: CERL ISTC ia01434500, Four sons of Aymon [English].
evidence.locator: title, translator, commission, imprint, references, and holdings
evidence.summary: Identifies the Middle English translation by William Caxton, commission by John, Earl of Oxford, Westminster printing about 1490, Manchester imperfect copy, and Cambridge fragment, while still reporting four Cambridge leaves.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Library, Early Printing Collection, Bookreader 19180 / reference 19180.
evidence.locator: item metadata and all 550 IIIF canvases
evidence.summary: The current record identifies the c. 1490 Westminster Middle English print, reference 19180, dimensions, provenance, and CC BY-NC-SA image terms. Visual inspection maps the target print to canvases 11-544: it begins at signature B.iii.a within Chapter I, contains documented internal losses, and ends with AMEN while the colophon remains lost.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, iDiscover record alma9948278273403606, Inc.3.J.1.1[3522].
evidence.locator: title, uniform title, imprint, commission, fragment note, provenance, and shelfmark
evidence.summary: The current record gives shelfmark Inc.3.J.1.1[3522] and states that only two leaves of gathering E remain, formerly four, with the other two missing since at least 1981. This supersedes but does not erase ISTC's current four-leaf statement.

evidence.citation: British Library current catalog record alma990001542870109251, C.12.i.7.
evidence.locator: title, 1554 Copland/Petet imprint, extent, holding, and shelfmark
evidence.summary: Identifies Richardson's exact 1554 supply copy as British Library C.12.i.7, printed by William Copland for Thomas Petet, with 174 numbered leaves.

evidence.citation: British Library current catalog record alma990001542610109251, IB.42244.
evidence.locator: title, historical 1480? date, 228-leaf extent, text opening and ending, and shelfmark
evidence.summary: The current record preserves the older Lyons 1480? description and identifies shelfmark IB.42244. Its repository, date, format, 228-leaf extent, missing first and last leaves, and textual opening and ending identify it as Richardson's French comparison volume; modern ISTC attributes the edition to the Printer of L'Abusé en court about 1485.

evidence.citation: Lotte Hellinga, 'From Poggio to Caxton: Early Translations of Some of Poggio's Latin Facetiae,' in Texts in Transit (Brill, 2014), pp. 258-259.
evidence.summary: Dates Caxton's Four Sons of Aymon about 1490 and states that his source may have been either the Guillaume Le Roy Lyon edition of about 1482-85 or the Printer of L'Abusé en court Lyon edition of about 1485; without extensive textual investigation, the choice cannot be decided.

evidence.citation: CERL ISTC ia01432800, Les quatre fils Aymon, Guillaume Le Roy edition.
evidence.locator: imprint, date alternatives, holdings, references, and digital copy
evidence.summary: Describes the Lyon Guillaume Le Roy French print as about 1482-85, with CIBN dating about 1485-86, and links the BnF RES-Y2-365 digital copy. It is one candidate source class, not an identified Caxton exemplar.

evidence.citation: CERL ISTC ia01432900, Les quatre fils Aymon, Printer of L'Abusé en court edition.
evidence.locator: imprint, date alternatives, holdings, references, and digital copy
evidence.summary: Describes the Lyon Printer of L'Abusé en court French print as about 1485, with CIBN dating about 1483-85, and lists BnF RES-Y2-364 and British Library IB.42244 among its copies. It is one candidate source class; the BL copy is Richardson's comparison witness, not thereby Caxton's exemplar.

evidence.citation: Université de Caen Normandie, Éditions Lyonnaises de Romans, [Renaut de Montauban] Quatre fils Aymon.
evidence.locator: work description and early Lyon editions
evidence.summary: Describes the anonymous prose vulgate as a medieval French adventure romance close to Arsenal 3151, records the two early Lyon incunables and current copies, and notes the traditional twenty-eight-chapter organization.

Supplies stable entity IDs and exact relations for the repository XML, U-M encoding, two Richardson issues, Caxton witnesses, Copland supplies, French comparison copy, candidate source editions, access, rights, and cached copies.

open_questions.question: Which physical French prose book did Caxton translate, and was it a copy of either early Lyon edition identified by Hellinga?
next_steps: Undertake or consult a full textual and typographical comparison of Caxton against both Lyon print states and the broader prose tradition.
Do not equate Richardson's British Library IB.42244 comparison copy with Caxton's exemplar without item-level provenance or textual proof.

open_questions.question: Can Caxton's translation and first printing be dated more narrowly than about 1490?
next_steps: Review current type, paper, and publishing-chronology evidence behind ISTC's about-1490 date.
Retain Richardson's historical about-1489 dating as evidence rather than silently replacing it.

open_questions.question: When and under what circumstances were two of Cambridge's formerly four gathering-E leaves lost?
next_steps: Consult Cambridge conservation, accession, and reader-use records before and after 1981.
Notify ISTC of the current two-leaf record if the discrepancy has not already entered its revision queue.

open_questions.question: Can separate physical title pages for both original Richardson issues be inspected to confirm the XML's 1885 and 1886 title-page dates against the EETS issue years 1884 and 1885?
next_steps: Inspect complete original wrappers and title leaves for ES 44 and ES 45 separately.
Keep issue year and title-page date as distinct assertions until both physical carriers have been checked.

notes: The cataloging boundary is Caxton's complete intended Middle English prose work, not the incomplete surviving Manchester copy and not Richardson's composite textual realization.

The Manchester IIIF object is complete as a 550-canvas digital representation of its present bound volume, but the physical witness is textually imperfect: its opening, three internal runs, colophon, and a small late cut-out are absent.
The Cambridge current holding has two leaves, while ISTC still says four. Both current assertions are preserved with their source dates rather than reconciled by guesswork.

Richardson's Part I and Part II are separate issue carriers. EETS lists issue years 1884 and 1885; the XML title-page field gives Roman-numeral dates 1885 and 1886. The combined cached scan preserves a title page dated 1885 and the page-321 transition into Part II but not a separately inspectable Part II title leaf.
